startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: A 55-year-old man presents with sudden-onset severe abdominal pain. Work through the imaging findings to reach the correct diagnosis and guide management.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You correctly interpreted the radiographic findings, identified the appropriate contrast agent, and understood CT density values. Outstanding radiodiagnosis knowledge.
You showed some radiological knowledge but missed key imaging principles. Review radiographic densities, contrast agents, and Hounsfield units.
Review the fundamentals of X-ray interpretation, contrast studies, and CT imaging. Focus on the five radiographic densities and indications for different contrast agents.

params.text:
Case Presentation

A 55-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset severe epigastric pain radiating to the back. He has a history of peptic ulcer disease. On examination, the abdomen is rigid with board-like rigidity and absent bowel sounds.

An erect chest X-ray is obtained.

The radiograph shows a crescent of lucency beneath both domes of the diaphragm. This finding represents free gas (pneumoperitoneum) — one of the five radiographic densities.

Consider what this finding indicates and the urgent next step.

branchingQuestion.question: Free gas under the diaphragm on an erect X-ray in this clinical context most likely indicates:

alternatives.text: Perforation of a hollow viscus — this is a surgical emergency requiring urgent intervention

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: Free gas under the diaphragm (pneumoperitoneum) in a patient with peptic ulcer disease and peritoneal signs indicates perforated peptic ulcer — a surgical emergency requiring urgent laparotomy or laparoscopic repair.

alternatives.text: Normal finding after a recent meal due to swallowed air

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Swallowed air is contained within the GI tract lumen and does not appear as free gas under the diaphragm. Free subdiaphragmatic air indicates gas has escaped from a perforated hollow viscus into the peritoneal cavity.

alternatives.text: Pleural effusion requiring chest drain insertion

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Pleural effusion appears as a fluid density (white) at the lung bases with a meniscus sign, not as lucency (black) under the diaphragm. The crescent of lucency here represents free gas, not fluid.

params.text:
Contrast Study Consideration

The surgical team requests a contrast study to confirm the site of perforation before surgery.

Important decision: When perforation is suspected, the choice of contrast agent is critical.

  • Barium sulfate: Excellent mucosal coating for routine GI studies, but causes severe chemical peritonitis if it leaks into the peritoneal cavity
  • Water-soluble iodine contrast (Gastrografin): Safe if it leaks into the peritoneum — rapidly absorbed and excreted by the kidneys

You must choose the appropriate contrast agent for this clinical scenario.

branchingQuestion.question: Which contrast agent should be used for this patient with suspected GI perforation?

alternatives.text: Water-soluble iodine contrast (Gastrografin) — safe in suspected perforation as it is absorbed from the peritoneum

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: Gastrografin is the contrast of choice when perforation is suspected. It is water-soluble, rapidly absorbed from the peritoneal cavity, and does not cause peritonitis. Barium is absolutely contraindicated as it causes severe granulomatous peritonitis.

alternatives.text: Barium sulfate — it provides the best mucosal detail for identifying the perforation site

feedback.title: Dangerous choice!
feedback.subtitle: Barium sulfate is contraindicated when perforation is suspected. If barium leaks into the peritoneal cavity, it causes severe barium peritonitis with high mortality. Always use water-soluble contrast (Gastrografin) when perforation is a possibility.

alternatives.text: No contrast needed — proceed directly to CT with Hounsfield unit measurements only

feedback.title: Partially correct
feedback.subtitle: CT without oral contrast can identify pneumoperitoneum and sometimes the perforation site. However, the question specifically asks about contrast choice. When oral contrast is needed in suspected perforation, water-soluble iodine contrast is the correct choice. CT with Hounsfield units helps quantify tissue density (bone ~+400 to +1000 HU, air ~-1000 HU).

params.text:
Key Learning Points

  • Five radiographic densities (most to least opaque): Metal/bone > soft tissue > fat > air > (foreign body varies)
  • Pneumoperitoneum: Free gas under the diaphragm on erect X-ray indicates perforation of a hollow viscus — a surgical emergency
  • Barium sulfate: Excellent for routine GI contrast studies but absolutely contraindicated when perforation is suspected (causes barium peritonitis)
  • Gastrografin: Water-soluble iodine contrast — safe in suspected perforation, absorbed from peritoneum
  • CT Hounsfield units: Quantify tissue density — bone (+400 to +1000 HU), soft tissue (+20 to +70 HU), fat (-50 to -100 HU), air (-1000 HU)
  • Erect chest X-ray: Most sensitive plain film for detecting pneumoperitoneum — as little as 1 mL of free air can be visible
